AP Physics C: Electricity & Magnetism

Unit 2 — Electric Potential

An interactive review of electric potential energy, electric potential, potential difference, the electron-volt, and equipotential surfaces — with worked solutions to the sample problems from your notes and exam-style practice. Track your review with the sidebar checkboxes and flag tricky questions with ⚑ Mark for review.

13 topics Worked sample problems Exam-style practice Formula sheet Diagrams from your notes
1

Electric Potential Energy

Lecture 0636

The electrostatic force is conservative, so — just like gravity — it has a potential energy associated with it. This is the energy stored in the arrangement of a system of charges.

Core Concepts

Imagine one charge \(q_1\) alone in the universe. Bring a second charge \(q_2\) from infinitely far away to a distance \(r\) at constant velocity; the work done by the external force equals the change in electric potential energy of the two-charge system. Taking \(U=0\) at infinite separation:

Electric potential energy of two point charges
\[ U_E=\frac{kq_1q_2}{r} \]
  • \(U_E\) = electric potential energy of the pair (J); zero when \(r\to\infty\)
  • \(q_1,q_2\) = the two charges with sign; \(r\) = their separation
  • Note: unlike Coulomb's force, this has \(r\) (not \(r^2\)) in the denominator
The sign of \(U_E\) comes straight from the charges
  • Like charges (both + or both −): \(U_E>0\). It takes positive external work to push them together.
  • Unlike charges: \(U_E<0\). They attract, so the external force does negative work bringing them together (it must hold them back).
Worked Example
Example PE of an attracting pair

Find the electric potential energy of a \(+3.0\,\mu\text{C}\) and a \(-3.0\,\mu\text{C}\) charge separated by \(0.20\ \text{m}\).

\[ U_E=\frac{kq_1q_2}{r}=\frac{(8.99\times10^9)(3.0\times10^{-6})(-3.0\times10^{-6})}{0.20}\approx -0.40\ \text{J} \]

The value is negative because the charges are unlike — the system is bound, and you'd have to add \(0.40\ \text{J}\) of work to pull them apart to infinity.

Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. The electric potential energy of two point charges is \(U\). If their separation is doubled, the new potential energy is:
  • A\(U/4\)
  • B\(U/2\)
  • C\(2U\)
  • D\(4U\)
Answer B

\(U_E\propto 1/r\) (not \(1/r^2\) like the force). Doubling \(r\) halves the energy.

MCQ
Q2. Two negative charges are held a fixed distance apart. The electric potential energy of the system is:
  • Apositive
  • Bnegative
  • Czero
  • Dimpossible to determine
Answer A

\(U_E=kq_1q_2/r\). Two negatives give \((-)(-)=+\), so \(U_E>0\). Like charges always have positive potential energy.

MCQ
Q3. Charges \(+4.0\,\mu\text{C}\) and \(+2.0\,\mu\text{C}\) are \(0.30\ \text{m}\) apart. Their electric potential energy is closest to:
  • A\(0.024\ \text{J}\)
  • B\(0.080\ \text{J}\)
  • C\(0.24\ \text{J}\)
  • D\(2.4\ \text{J}\)
Answer C
\[ U_E=\frac{(8.99\times10^9)(4.0\times10^{-6})(2.0\times10^{-6})}{0.30}\approx +0.24\ \text{J} \]
FRQ
Q4. A \(+3.0\,\mu\text{C}\) charge is fixed in place. How much work must an external force do to bring a \(+5.0\,\mu\text{C}\) charge from very far away to a point \(0.40\ \text{m}\) from it (at constant speed)? Is the work positive or negative, and why?
Solution \(W_{\text{ext}}=+0.34\ \text{J}\)

At constant speed, \(W_{\text{ext}}=\Delta U=U_f-U_i\). With \(U_i=0\) (infinite separation):

\[ W_{\text{ext}}=\frac{kq_1q_2}{r}=\frac{(8.99\times10^9)(3.0\times10^{-6})(5.0\times10^{-6})}{0.40}\approx +0.34\ \text{J} \]

It is positive: the charges are alike and repel, so the external force must push the incoming charge inward, doing positive work and storing energy in the system.

2

Potential Energy of Multiple Charges

Lecture 0639

Because energy is a scalar, the total potential energy of a group of charges is just the sum of the energies of every pair — no vectors, no components.

Core Concept

Build the system one charge at a time. Each new charge interacts with every charge already present, and each interaction contributes \(kq_iq_j/r_{ij}\). Add up every unique pair:

Total potential energy of a system
\[ U_{\text{total}}=k\sum_{\text{pairs}}\frac{q_iq_j}{r_{ij}}=k\!\left(\frac{q_1q_2}{r_{12}}+\frac{q_1q_3}{r_{13}}+\frac{q_2q_3}{r_{23}}+\cdots\right) \]
Counting pairsA 3-charge system has 3 pairs; a 4-charge system has 6. In general \(N\) charges give \(\binom{N}{2}=\dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}\) pairs.
Worked Example — the 3-charge triangle (from your notes)
Example \(q_1=+6.0\,\mu\text{C}\), \(q_2=-5.0\,\mu\text{C}\), \(q_3=+4.0\,\mu\text{C}\), equilateral, side \(1.0\ \text{m}\)

Compute each pair energy (all \(r=1.0\ \text{m}\)):

\[ U_{12}=\frac{k(6)(-5)}{1.0}\times10^{-12}\cdot 10^{9}=-0.27\ \text{J} \]
\[ U_{13}=k\frac{(6\times10^{-6})(4\times10^{-6})}{1.0}=+0.22\ \text{J},\qquad U_{23}=k\frac{(-5\times10^{-6})(4\times10^{-6})}{1.0}=-0.18\ \text{J} \]

Add the three scalars:

\[ U_{\text{total}}=U_{12}+U_{13}+U_{23}=-0.27+0.22-0.18\approx -0.23\ \text{J} \]
InterpretationThe two attractive (unlike) pairs store negative energy; the one repulsive (like) pair stores positive energy. The net is negative, so this arrangement is bound relative to the charges being infinitely far apart.
Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. How many distinct pair interactions contribute to the total potential energy of a four-charge system?
  • A3
  • B4
  • C6
  • D12
Answer C

\(\binom{4}{2}=\dfrac{4\cdot3}{2}=6\) pairs: (12),(13),(14),(23),(24),(34).

MCQ
Q2. When finding the total potential energy of several charges, you may add the pair energies as simple numbers (no vector components) because electric potential energy is:
  • Aa vector
  • Ba scalar
  • Calways positive
  • Dindependent of charge sign
Answer B

Energy is a scalar, so the pair energies add directly (carrying their signs). This is a big simplification compared with adding force or field vectors.

FRQ
Q3. Three charges sit at the corners of an equilateral triangle of side \(0.50\ \text{m}\): \(q_1=+2.0\,\mu\text{C}\), \(q_2=+2.0\,\mu\text{C}\), \(q_3=-2.0\,\mu\text{C}\). Find the total electric potential energy of the system.
Solution \(U_{\text{total}}\approx -0.072\ \text{J}\)

With all \(r=0.50\ \text{m}\), factor out \(k/r\):

\[ U_{\text{total}}=\frac{k}{r}\big(q_1q_2+q_1q_3+q_2q_3\big) \]
\[ =\frac{8.99\times10^9}{0.50}\big[(2)(2)+(2)(-2)+(2)(-2)\big]\times10^{-12}=\frac{8.99\times10^9}{0.50}(-4\times10^{-12})\approx -0.072\ \text{J} \]

The two attractive pairs outweigh the single repulsive pair, so the total is negative.

FRQ
Q4. Two charges \(+q\) are fixed a distance \(d\) apart. A third charge \(+q\) is brought from infinity to form an equilateral triangle of side \(d\). Derive a symbolic expression for the work the external force must do.
Solution \(W_{\text{ext}}=\dfrac{2kq^2}{d}\)

The work equals the change in the system's potential energy, which is the energy of the new pairs the third charge forms (the first pair already existed):

\[ W_{\text{ext}}=U_{13}+U_{23}=\frac{kq^2}{d}+\frac{kq^2}{d}=\frac{2kq^2}{d} \]

Positive, since the incoming charge is repelled by both fixed charges.

3

Reading the \(U\)-vs-\(r\) Graph

Lecture 0640

A single graph of potential energy versus separation answers most of the "why" questions about how charges move.

The Graph

Plotting \(U_E=\dfrac{kq_1q_2}{r}\) against \(r\) gives two branches depending on the signs of the charges:

  • Like charges (positive \(U\)): \(U\) is large at small \(r\) and decreases toward \(0\) as \(r\to\infty\).
  • Unlike charges (negative \(U\)): \(U\) is very negative at small \(r\) and rises toward \(0\) as \(r\to\infty\).
The master ruleIn the absence of external forces, a system always accelerates toward lower potential energy (converting \(U\) into kinetic energy). The force is the negative slope, \(F=-\dfrac{dU}{dr}\).
  • Like charges reach lowest \(U\) (\(=0\)) by flying apart to infinity.
  • Unlike charges reach lowest \(U\) (toward \(-\infty\)) by coming together.
Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. Two charges are released from rest. In the absence of other forces, the system will move so as to:
  • Aincrease its potential energy
  • Bdecrease its potential energy
  • Ckeep its potential energy constant
  • Dmaximize the distance regardless of sign
Answer B

Systems move toward lower potential energy, converting \(U\) into kinetic energy — exactly like a dropped ball moving to lower gravitational \(U\).

MCQ
Q2. For two like charges, the lowest-energy configuration corresponds to:
  • Athe charges touching (\(r\to0\))
  • Ba separation of exactly \(1\ \text{m}\)
  • Cinfinite separation (\(U\to 0\))
  • Dthere is no lowest-energy state
Answer C

Like charges have positive \(U=kq_1q_2/r\) that decreases as \(r\) grows, reaching its minimum value of \(0\) at infinite separation — so they fly apart.

MCQ
Q3. On a \(U\)-vs-\(r\) graph, the magnitude of the force between the charges at a given separation is represented by:
  • Athe steepness (slope) of the curve there
  • Bthe value of \(U\) there
  • Cthe area under the curve
  • Dthe \(r\)-intercept
Answer A

\(F=-\dfrac{dU}{dr}\): the force is the negative slope of the \(U\)-vs-\(r\) curve. A steeper curve means a stronger force.

FRQ
Q4. Explain, using the definition of \(U_E\) and the reference at infinity, why the electric potential energy of two unlike charges is always negative.
Solution

We define \(U_E=0\) when the charges are infinitely far apart. For unlike charges, \(q_1q_2<0\), so \(U_E=kq_1q_2/r<0\) at any finite separation. Equivalently: the charges attract, so as they approach from infinity they speed up — gaining kinetic energy that must come from a decrease in potential energy below the zero reference. Since \(U_E\) starts at \(0\) and only decreases as they get closer, it is always negative for unlike charges.

4

Change in Potential Energy in a Uniform Field

Lecture 0671

Between parallel plates the field is uniform, so the potential energy changes in a simple, linear way — exactly analogous to \(\Delta U_g=mg\,\Delta y\) near Earth's surface.

Core Concept

A conservative force and its potential energy are linked by \(\Delta U=-W_{\text{field}}\). In a uniform field \(E\), the electric force on a charge is \(F=qE\), so moving the charge a displacement \(\Delta r\) parallel to the field changes the charge–plates system's energy by:

Uniform-field potential energy change
\[ \Delta U=-qE\,\Delta r \qquad(\text{displacement }\Delta r\text{ along }\vec E) \]
  • \(E\) = uniform field magnitude; \(\Delta r\) = distance moved parallel to \(\vec E\)
  • For a positive charge moving along \(\vec E\): \(\Delta U<0\) (energy released as KE)
  • For a negative charge moving along \(\vec E\): \(\Delta U>0\)
Gravitational analogyLifting a mass up (against \(\vec g\)) gives \(\Delta U_g=+mg\,\Delta y\). Likewise, pushing a positive charge against \(\vec E\) raises its potential energy; letting it move with \(\vec E\) lowers it.
Worked Example
Example Energy released by an accelerating charge

A \(+2.0\,\mu\text{C}\) charge moves \(0.050\ \text{m}\) in the direction of a uniform \(1000\ \text{N/C}\) field. Find \(\Delta U\).

\[ \Delta U=-qE\,\Delta r=-(2.0\times10^{-6})(1000)(0.050)=-1.0\times10^{-4}\ \text{J} \]

The potential energy drops by \(1.0\times10^{-4}\ \text{J}\); if no other force acts, that energy appears as kinetic energy.

Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. A positive charge moves a distance \(d\) in the direction of a uniform electric field. Its electric potential energy:
  • Aincreases
  • Bdecreases
  • Cstays the same
  • Dbecomes zero
Answer B

The field does positive work on a positive charge moving along \(\vec E\), so \(\Delta U=-W<0\): the energy decreases (just like a ball falling).

MCQ
Q2. A negative charge moves a distance \(d\) in the direction of a uniform field. Its potential energy:
  • Aincreases
  • Bdecreases
  • Cstays the same
  • Dcannot be determined
Answer A

The force on a negative charge is opposite \(\vec E\), so moving it along \(\vec E\) is moving it against the force — the field does negative work and \(\Delta U>0\).

MCQ
Q3. A \(+3.0\,\mu\text{C}\) charge moves \(0.10\ \text{m}\) along a uniform \(500\ \text{N/C}\) field. The change in potential energy is:
  • A\(+1.5\times10^{-4}\ \text{J}\)
  • B\(0\)
  • C\(-1.5\times10^{-4}\ \text{J}\)
  • D\(-1.5\times10^{-2}\ \text{J}\)
Answer C
\[ \Delta U=-qE\,\Delta r=-(3.0\times10^{-6})(500)(0.10)=-1.5\times10^{-4}\ \text{J} \]
FRQ
Q4. A charge \(+q\) of mass \(m\) is released from rest in a uniform field \(E\) and moves a distance \(d\) in the field direction. Derive expressions for (a) the kinetic energy gained and (b) its final speed.
Solution \(KE=qEd\); \(v=\sqrt{\dfrac{2qEd}{m}}\)

(a) Energy conservation: the kinetic energy gained equals the potential energy lost, \(KE=-\Delta U=qEd\).

(b) \(\tfrac12 mv^2=qEd\Rightarrow v=\sqrt{\dfrac{2qEd}{m}}.\)

5

Electric Potential (V)

Lectures 0658, 0660

Electric potential is the electric potential energy per unit charge at a point — a property of the field itself, whether or not a charge is sitting there.

Core Concepts

Just as the electric field is force per unit charge, the electric potential is energy per unit charge. It is a scalar measured in volts (\(1\ \text{V}=1\ \text{J/C}\)).

Definition & point-charge potential
\[ V=\frac{U}{q}\qquad\text{and, for a point charge,}\qquad V=\frac{kq}{r} \]
  • \(V\) = electric potential (V = J/C), a scalar that can be \(+\) or \(-\)
  • Reference: \(V=0\) infinitely far from the charge
  • Superposition: for several charges, \(V=\sum \dfrac{kq_i}{r_i}\) (just add the scalars)
Potential vs. potential energyElectric potential \(V\) belongs to a location in the field. Electric potential energy \(U=qV\) appears only once you place a charge \(q\) there. As the lecture puts it: "electric potential is the potential to have electric potential energy."
Worked Examples (from your notes)
Example A Potential energy of a pair

Charges \(+6.0\,\mu\text{C}\) and \(+9.0\,\mu\text{C}\) are \(3.0\ \text{m}\) apart. Find the system's potential energy.

\[ U=\frac{kq_1q_2}{r}=\frac{(8.99\times10^9)(6.0\times10^{-6})(9.0\times10^{-6})}{3.0}\approx 0.16\ \text{J} \]
Example B Potential from one charge

What is the electric potential \(3.0\ \text{m}\) from the \(+6.0\,\mu\text{C}\) charge alone?

\[ V=\frac{kq}{r}=\frac{(8.99\times10^9)(6.0\times10^{-6})}{3.0}\approx 1.8\times10^{4}\ \text{V}=18\ \text{kV} \]

Check: placing the \(+9.0\,\mu\text{C}\) charge at that spot gives \(U=qV=(9.0\times10^{-6})(1.8\times10^4)\approx 0.16\ \text{J}\) — matching Example A. Knowing \(V\) makes the energy for any charge a one-step multiply.

Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. Electric potential is best described as a:
  • Ascalar (can be positive or negative)
  • Bvector pointing along \(\vec E\)
  • Cvector pointing opposite \(\vec E\)
  • Dalways-positive scalar
Answer A

\(V=U/q\) is a scalar; its sign matches the sign of the source charge (positive near \(+\), negative near \(-\)). Being a scalar is what makes superposition so easy.

MCQ
Q2. The electric potential a distance \(0.30\ \text{m}\) from a \(+5.0\ \text{nC}\) point charge is closest to:
  • A\(15\ \text{V}\)
  • B\(150\ \text{V}\)
  • C\(500\ \text{V}\)
  • D\(1500\ \text{V}\)
Answer B
\[ V=\frac{kq}{r}=\frac{(8.99\times10^9)(5.0\times10^{-9})}{0.30}\approx 150\ \text{V} \]
MCQ
Q3. At a point where the electric potential is \(200\ \text{V}\), the electric potential energy of a \(+3.0\,\mu\text{C}\) charge placed there is:
  • A\(67\ \text{J}\)
  • B\(6.0\times10^{-3}\ \text{J}\)
  • C\(6.0\times10^{-4}\ \text{J}\)
  • D\(600\ \text{J}\)
Answer C

\(U=qV=(3.0\times10^{-6})(200)=6.0\times10^{-4}\ \text{J}.\)

FRQ
Q4. A \(+8.0\,\mu\text{C}\) point charge is fixed in place. (a) Find the electric potential \(2.0\ \text{m}\) away. (b) Find the potential energy if a \(-3.0\,\mu\text{C}\) charge is placed at that point, and comment on its sign.
Solution (a) \(\approx 3.6\times10^{4}\ \text{V}\); (b) \(\approx -0.11\ \text{J}\)

(a) \(V=\dfrac{kq}{r}=\dfrac{(8.99\times10^9)(8.0\times10^{-6})}{2.0}\approx 3.6\times10^{4}\ \text{V}.\)

(b) \(U=qV=(-3.0\times10^{-6})(3.6\times10^4)\approx -0.11\ \text{J}.\) Negative, because a negative charge in a positive potential forms a bound (attractive) pair.

6

Electric Potential Difference

Lectures 0470-1, 0672

In practice we rarely know the absolute potential at a point — but we very often know the difference between two points, and that's what determines energy changes.

Core Concepts

The electric potential difference between points A and B is the difference in their potentials, and it is tied to the field by a line integral:

Potential difference & its link to the field
\[ \Delta V=V_B-V_A=-\int_A^B \vec E\cdot d\vec l \qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad \vec E=-\frac{dV}{dr} \]
Energy change of a charge
\[ \Delta U=q\,\Delta V \]
What the negative sign meansThe electric field points in the direction of decreasing potential. Moving with the field, \(\Delta V<0\); moving against it, \(\Delta V>0\). Because \(E=-dV/dr\), field units can also be written as volts per meter (\(\text{V/m}=\text{N/C}\)).
Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. Which of these is an equivalent unit for electric field strength?
  • A\(\text{J/C}\)
  • B\(\text{V}\cdot\text{m}\)
  • C\(\text{V/m}\)
  • D\(\text{C/N}\)
Answer C

Since \(E=-dV/dr\), the field has units of volts per meter, \(\text{V/m}\), which is identical to \(\text{N/C}\). (\(\text{J/C}\) is the volt — a unit of potential, not field.)

MCQ
Q2. A \(+2.0\,\mu\text{C}\) charge is moved through a potential difference of \(\Delta V=+50\ \text{V}\). The change in its potential energy is:
  • A\(+2.5\times10^{-8}\ \text{J}\)
  • B\(+1.0\times10^{-4}\ \text{J}\)
  • C\(-1.0\times10^{-4}\ \text{J}\)
  • D\(+100\ \text{J}\)
Answer B

\(\Delta U=q\,\Delta V=(2.0\times10^{-6})(+50)=+1.0\times10^{-4}\ \text{J}.\)

MCQ
Q3. In a region of space, in which direction does the electric field point relative to the electric potential?
  • Atoward increasing potential
  • Btoward decreasing potential
  • Calong surfaces of constant potential
  • Dthere is no relationship
Answer B

From \(\vec E=-dV/dr\), the field points "downhill" in potential — toward decreasing \(V\).

FRQ
Q4. State the relationship between electric potential difference and the electric field, and explain the meaning of the negative sign in \(\Delta V=-\int \vec E\cdot d\vec l\).
Solution

The potential difference between two points is the negative line integral of the field along any path between them: \(\Delta V=-\int_A^B \vec E\cdot d\vec l\). Equivalently, the field is the negative spatial derivative of potential, \(\vec E=-dV/dr\). The negative sign encodes that the field points toward decreasing potential: if you move in the direction of \(\vec E\), the dot product is positive and \(\Delta V\) comes out negative (potential drops). Because the force is conservative, the result is path-independent.

7

Work to Move a Charge & the Electron-Volt

Lecture 0470-2

Moving a charge through a potential difference takes (or releases) energy. For tiny atomic-scale energies, we use a convenient unit: the electron-volt.

Core Concepts

If an external force moves a charge from A to B with no change in kinetic energy, the work it does equals the change in electric potential energy:

Work and the electron-volt
\[ W=q\,\Delta V \qquad\text{and}\qquad 1\ \text{eV}=(1.60\times10^{-19}\,\text{C})(1\ \text{V})=1.60\times10^{-19}\ \text{J} \]
Two common trapsThe electron-volt is a unit of energy, not potential — despite the name. And one electron-volt is a positive amount of energy, even though the electron's charge is negative. It is simply the energy a charge of magnitude \(e\) gains or loses crossing a \(1\ \text{V}\) difference.
Worked Example
Example Energy from an accelerating voltage

An electron is accelerated from rest through a potential difference of magnitude \(100\ \text{V}\). How much kinetic energy does it gain, in eV and in joules?

\[ \Delta KE=|q\,\Delta V|=(e)(100\ \text{V})=100\ \text{eV}=100\times1.60\times10^{-19}=1.6\times10^{-17}\ \text{J} \]

Using eV makes the bookkeeping trivial: a charge of magnitude \(e\) through \(100\ \text{V}\) is just \(100\ \text{eV}\).

Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. The electron-volt (eV) is a unit of:
  • Aelectric potential
  • Benergy
  • Ccharge
  • Delectric field
Answer B

Despite the misleading name, \(1\ \text{eV}=1.60\times10^{-19}\ \text{J}\) is a (small) unit of energy.

MCQ
Q2. A proton is accelerated through a potential difference of \(500\ \text{V}\). The kinetic energy it gains is:
  • A\(500\ \text{J}\)
  • B\(1.6\times10^{-19}\ \text{J}\)
  • C\(8.0\times10^{-17}\ \text{J}\) (i.e. \(500\ \text{eV}\))
  • D\(3.1\times10^{21}\ \text{J}\)
Answer C

\(\Delta KE=q\Delta V=e(500\ \text{V})=500\ \text{eV}=500\times1.60\times10^{-19}=8.0\times10^{-17}\ \text{J}.\)

FRQ
Q3. An electron starts from rest and is accelerated through a potential difference of magnitude \(200\ \text{V}\). (a) Find its final kinetic energy in eV and in joules. (b) Find its final speed. (\(m_e=9.11\times10^{-31}\ \text{kg}\))
Solution (a) \(200\ \text{eV}=3.2\times10^{-17}\ \text{J}\); (b) \(v\approx 8.4\times10^{6}\ \text{m/s}\)

(a) \(\Delta KE=e(200\ \text{V})=200\ \text{eV}=200\times1.60\times10^{-19}=3.2\times10^{-17}\ \text{J}.\)

(b) \(\tfrac12 m_e v^2=3.2\times10^{-17}\Rightarrow v=\sqrt{\dfrac{2(3.2\times10^{-17})}{9.11\times10^{-31}}}\approx 8.4\times10^{6}\ \text{m/s}.\)

8

Potential Difference in a Uniform Field

Lectures 0672, 0470-3

Between parallel plates the field is constant, so the line integral collapses to a simple product — giving the most-used relationship in this unit: \(E=V/d\).

Core Concepts

For a uniform field, \(\Delta V=-\int \vec E\cdot d\vec l\) becomes:

Uniform field
\[ \Delta V=-E\,\Delta r \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad E=\frac{|\Delta V|}{d}=\frac{V}{d} \]
  • \(\Delta r\) (or plate spacing \(d\)) = distance measured parallel to \(\vec E\)
  • \(E\) between parallel plates is uniform; the potential drops steadily from the \(+\) plate to the \(-\) plate
  • If the field is not uniform, this gives the average field: \(E_{\text{avg}}=-\Delta V/\Delta r\)
Worked Example (from your notes)
Example Field between plates

Two parallel plates have a potential difference of \(12\ \text{V}\) and are separated by \(1.0\ \text{cm}\). Find the electric field between them.

\[ E=\frac{V}{d}=\frac{12\ \text{V}}{0.010\ \text{m}}=1.2\times10^{3}\ \text{V/m} \]

The field points from the positive plate toward the negative plate — the direction of decreasing potential.

Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. Two parallel plates are separated by \(0.020\ \text{m}\) with a \(100\ \text{V}\) potential difference. The field between them is:
  • A\(2.0\ \text{V/m}\)
  • B\(50\ \text{V/m}\)
  • C\(5.0\times10^{3}\ \text{V/m}\)
  • D\(2.0\times10^{3}\ \text{V/m}\)
Answer C

\(E=\dfrac{V}{d}=\dfrac{100}{0.020}=5.0\times10^{3}\ \text{V/m}.\)

MCQ
Q2. For a fixed potential difference across two plates, if the plate separation is doubled, the field between the plates:
  • Adoubles
  • Bis halved
  • Cis unchanged
  • Dquadruples
Answer B

\(E=V/d\): with \(V\) fixed, \(E\propto 1/d\). Doubling \(d\) halves \(E\).

MCQ
Q3. As a positive charge moves from the positive plate toward the negative plate, the electric potential:
  • Adecreases
  • Bincreases
  • Cstays constant
  • Dfirst increases, then decreases
Answer A

The field points from \(+\) plate to \(-\) plate, and potential always decreases in the field direction. So moving that way, \(V\) drops.

FRQ
Q4. Two parallel plates have a \(50\ \text{V}\) potential difference and are \(2.0\ \text{mm}\) apart. (a) Find the electric field between them. (b) Find the magnitude of the force on a \(+4.0\,\mu\text{C}\) charge placed between the plates.
Solution (a) \(2.5\times10^{4}\ \text{V/m}\); (b) \(0.10\ \text{N}\)

(a) \(E=\dfrac{V}{d}=\dfrac{50}{2.0\times10^{-3}}=2.5\times10^{4}\ \text{V/m}.\)

(b) \(F=qE=(4.0\times10^{-6})(2.5\times10^4)=0.10\ \text{N}.\)

9

Potential Difference Near a Point Charge

Lecture 0674

For a point charge the potential goes as \(kq/r\), so the difference between two locations is a clean two-term expression.

Core Concepts
Potential difference between two radii
\[ \Delta V=V_B-V_A=kq\!\left(\frac{1}{r_B}-\frac{1}{r_A}\right) \]
  • Electric potential increases as you move toward a positive charge (and decreases moving away).
  • Electric potential decreases as you move toward a negative charge.
  • Because the force is conservative, \(\Delta V\) is path-independent — all points on the same equipotential sphere (radius \(r\)) are at the same potential.
Worked Example
Example Moving outward from a positive charge

For a \(+5.0\ \text{nC}\) charge, find \(\Delta V\) going from \(r_A=0.10\ \text{m}\) to \(r_B=0.20\ \text{m}\).

\[ \Delta V=kq\!\left(\frac{1}{r_B}-\frac{1}{r_A}\right)=(8.99\times10^9)(5.0\times10^{-9})\!\left(\frac{1}{0.20}-\frac{1}{0.10}\right) \]
\[ =44.95\,(5.0-10.0)=-2.2\times10^{2}\ \text{V} \]

The potential drops by about \(225\ \text{V}\) as we move away from the positive charge — consistent with "potential decreases moving away from a positive charge."

Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. As you move closer to an isolated positive point charge, the electric potential:
  • Aincreases
  • Bdecreases
  • Cstays constant
  • Dbecomes negative
Answer A

\(V=kq/r\) with \(q>0\): as \(r\) decreases, \(V\) increases (toward \(+\infty\) at the charge).

MCQ
Q2. The potential difference between two points near a point charge depends on:
  • Athe specific path taken between them
  • Bthe speed of the test charge
  • Conly the two radii \(r_A\) and \(r_B\)
  • Dthe mass placed at the points
Answer C

\(\Delta V=kq(1/r_B-1/r_A)\) depends only on the endpoints' radii. The electrostatic force is conservative, so \(\Delta V\) is path-independent.

FRQ
Q3. A \(+6.0\ \text{nC}\) point charge is fixed in place. (a) Find the potential difference \(\Delta V=V_B-V_A\) between point A at \(r_A=0.20\ \text{m}\) and point B at \(r_B=0.50\ \text{m}\). (b) Find the change in potential energy of a \(+2.0\ \text{nC}\) charge carried from A to B.
Solution (a) \(\approx -1.6\times10^{2}\ \text{V}\); (b) \(\approx -3.2\times10^{-7}\ \text{J}\)

(a) \(\Delta V=kq\!\left(\dfrac{1}{0.50}-\dfrac{1}{0.20}\right)=(8.99\times10^9)(6.0\times10^{-9})(2.0-5.0)\approx -1.6\times10^{2}\ \text{V}.\)

(b) \(\Delta U=q\,\Delta V=(2.0\times10^{-9})(-162)\approx -3.2\times10^{-7}\ \text{J}.\) Potential energy decreases as the positive charge moves outward.

10

Speed of a Charged Particle

Lectures 0470-4, 0672

Put potential difference to work: when a charge accelerates through a \(\Delta V\), conservation of energy gives its speed directly.

Core Concept

With only the conservative electric force acting, mechanical energy is conserved. A charge released from rest converts its lost potential energy entirely into kinetic energy:

From rest through a potential difference
\[ \tfrac12 m v^2=|q\,\Delta V|\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad v=\sqrt{\frac{2|q\,\Delta V|}{m}} \]
Sign & direction checkA positive charge accelerates from the \(+\) plate toward the \(-\) plate (with the field). A negative charge released at the \(+\) plate would not move toward the \(-\) plate — it accelerates toward the \(+\) plate. Always confirm the charge actually speeds up in the direction you assumed.
Worked Example (from your notes)
Example Proton across a \(12\ \text{V}\) gap

A proton (\(q=1.60\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}\), \(m=1.67\times10^{-27}\ \text{kg}\)) is released from rest at the positive plate, where the plates differ by \(12\ \text{V}\). Find its speed just before hitting the negative plate.

\[ \tfrac12 m v^2=q|\Delta V| \;\Rightarrow\; v=\sqrt{\frac{2q|\Delta V|}{m}}=\sqrt{\frac{2(1.60\times10^{-19})(12)}{1.67\times10^{-27}}} \]
\[ v=\sqrt{2.30\times10^{9}}\approx 4.8\times10^{4}\ \text{m/s} \]

Notice the plate spacing was not needed — only the potential difference and the proton's charge and mass matter for the speed.

Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. A charged particle is accelerated from rest through a potential difference \(\Delta V\). Its final speed is proportional to:
  • A\(\Delta V\)
  • B\(\sqrt{\Delta V}\)
  • C\(\Delta V^2\)
  • D\(1/\Delta V\)
Answer B

\(v=\sqrt{2q\Delta V/m}\), so \(v\propto\sqrt{\Delta V}\). Quadrupling the voltage only doubles the speed.

MCQ
Q2. A proton and a deuteron (same charge, about twice the mass) are each accelerated from rest through the same \(\Delta V\). Compared with the proton, the deuteron's final speed is:
  • Atwice as large
  • Bthe same
  • Csmaller by a factor of \(\sqrt2\)
  • Dsmaller by a factor of 2
Answer C

Same \(q\) and \(\Delta V\) means the same kinetic energy, but \(v\propto1/\sqrt{m}\). Twice the mass gives a speed smaller by \(\sqrt2\).

MCQ
Q3. A proton (charge \(+e\)) and an alpha particle (charge \(+2e\)) accelerate from rest through the same potential difference. Which gains more kinetic energy?
  • Athe proton
  • Bthe alpha particle
  • Cthey gain equal kinetic energy
  • Dneither gains kinetic energy
Answer B

\(\Delta KE=q\,\Delta V\). The alpha has twice the charge, so it gains twice the kinetic energy (though, being heavier, not necessarily twice the speed).

FRQ
Q4. An alpha particle (\(q=2e=3.2\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}\), \(m=6.64\times10^{-27}\ \text{kg}\)) is accelerated from rest through a potential difference of magnitude \(1000\ \text{V}\). Find (a) its kinetic energy and (b) its final speed.
Solution (a) \(3.2\times10^{-16}\ \text{J}\); (b) \(\approx 3.1\times10^{5}\ \text{m/s}\)

(a) \(\Delta KE=q|\Delta V|=(3.2\times10^{-19})(1000)=3.2\times10^{-16}\ \text{J}.\)

(b) \(v=\sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\Delta KE}{m}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2(3.2\times10^{-16})}{6.64\times10^{-27}}}\approx 3.1\times10^{5}\ \text{m/s}.\)

11

Is the Change in Gravitational PE Really Negligible?

Lecture 0673

We routinely ignore gravity for charged particles in fields. Here's the quantitative justification — and why mass still matters.

Core Concept

Compare the energy a charge gains from the electric field to what it gains from gravity over the same displacement \(d\) (taking \(\vec E\) and \(\vec g\) parallel for simplicity). The ratio is independent of \(d\):

\[ \frac{|\Delta U_E|}{|\Delta U_g|}=\frac{qEd}{mgd}=\frac{qE}{mg} \]
Mass doesn't disappearEven though gravitational energy is negligible, the particle's mass stays in the speed equation \(v=\sqrt{2q\Delta V/m}\), because mass measures inertia — resistance to acceleration. More mass means a slower final speed for the same energy.
Worked Example
Example Proton in a \(1.0\times10^{4}\ \text{V/m}\) field

For a proton (\(q=1.6\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}\), \(m=1.67\times10^{-27}\ \text{kg}\)) in \(E=1.0\times10^{4}\ \text{V/m}\):

\[ \frac{|\Delta U_E|}{|\Delta U_g|}=\frac{qE}{mg}=\frac{(1.6\times10^{-19})(1.0\times10^{4})}{(1.67\times10^{-27})(9.8)}\approx 1\times10^{11} \]

The electric energy change is roughly 100 billion times larger than the gravitational one. That is the very definition of negligible.

Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. Why can we usually ignore gravity when analyzing a proton accelerating between charged plates?
  • AProtons have no mass.
  • BGravity does not act on charges.
  • CThe electric energy change is enormously larger than the gravitational one.
  • DThe plates block gravity.
Answer C

\(|\Delta U_E|/|\Delta U_g|=qE/(mg)\sim 10^{11}\) for a proton in a typical field. The gravitational contribution is utterly swamped.

MCQ
Q2. Since gravitational energy is negligible, why does the particle's mass still appear in \(v=\sqrt{2q\Delta V/m}\)?
  • AMass is the particle's inertia — its resistance to acceleration.
  • BIt's a mistake; mass should cancel.
  • CBecause gravity is not actually negligible.
  • DBecause mass equals charge for small particles.
Answer A

The kinetic energy gained equals \(q\Delta V\) regardless of mass, but converting that energy into speed depends on inertia: \(\tfrac12 mv^2=q\Delta V\). Larger mass ⇒ smaller speed.

FRQ
Q3. A proton moves through a uniform field \(E=2.0\times10^{3}\ \text{V/m}\). By computing the ratio \(|\Delta U_E|/|\Delta U_g|\), show that the change in gravitational potential energy is negligible. (\(q=1.6\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}\), \(m=1.67\times10^{-27}\ \text{kg}\))
Solution ratio \(\approx 2\times10^{10}\)

The displacement cancels, leaving

\[ \frac{|\Delta U_E|}{|\Delta U_g|}=\frac{qE}{mg}=\frac{(1.6\times10^{-19})(2.0\times10^{3})}{(1.67\times10^{-27})(9.8)}\approx 2\times10^{10}. \]

The electric energy change is about \(20\) billion times larger, so \(\Delta U_g\) is negligible.

12

Equipotential Surfaces

Lectures 0470-5, 0659, 0677

An equipotential surface connects all the points at the same potential — a contour map of the electric landscape, always crossing the field lines at right angles.

Core Properties
  • Every point on an equipotential surface has the same potential \(V\).
  • Equipotentials are always perpendicular (90°) to the electric field — so the field has no component along the surface.
  • It takes zero work to move a charge (at constant speed) along an equipotential, since \(\Delta V=0\) (and the force is perpendicular to the motion).
  • Field lines point in the direction of decreasing potential.
  • A field map and an equipotential map can each be constructed from the other.
Constant speed, not constant velocityMoving a charge along a curved equipotential (like a sphere around a point charge) takes zero net work, but the velocity direction changes — so it is constant speed, not constant velocity.
Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. Equipotential surfaces are always oriented how, relative to electric field lines?
  • Aparallel
  • Bat \(45^\circ\)
  • Cperpendicular (\(90^\circ\))
  • Dat an angle that depends on the charge
Answer C

If the field had any component along the surface, the potential would change along it — contradicting "equal potential." So \(\vec E\perp\) equipotential everywhere.

MCQ
Q2. How much work is required to move a charge at constant speed along an equipotential surface?
  • Azero
  • B\(qV\)
  • C\(qEd\)
  • Dit depends on the path length
Answer A

\(W=q\Delta V\), and \(\Delta V=0\) along an equipotential, so \(W=0\). (Equivalently, the electric force is perpendicular to the displacement.)

MCQ
Q3. The equipotential surfaces of a uniform field between parallel plates are:
  • Aconcentric spheres
  • Bflat planes parallel to the plates
  • Cplanes perpendicular to the plates
  • Dradial lines
Answer B

The field is uniform and perpendicular to the plates, so equipotentials are planes parallel to the plates (perpendicular to \(\vec E\)).

FRQ
Q4. Explain, in two different ways, why it takes zero work to move a charge along an equipotential surface.
Solution

Energy argument: the work done by the electric force is \(W=q\,\Delta V\). On an equipotential, all points have the same \(V\), so \(\Delta V=0\) and therefore \(W=0\).

Force/geometry argument: the field (and thus the electric force) is everywhere perpendicular to an equipotential. Since the displacement lies along the surface, the force is perpendicular to the displacement, and \(W=Fd\cos90^\circ=0\).

13

Finding the Field from the Potential

Lecture 0470-7

For a continuous charge distribution, it's often far easier to find the scalar potential first, then differentiate to get the field — no vector components to chase.

Core Concept

Because \(V\) is a scalar, the potential from a continuous distribution is a single scalar integral. Once you have \(V\) as a function of position, the field is its negative derivative:

Two-step strategy
\[ V=\int \frac{k\,dQ}{r}\qquad\text{then}\qquad E=-\frac{dV}{dx} \]
Why this is easierFinding \(E\) directly means adding field vectors (tracking components and angles). Finding \(V\) first means adding scalars. For symmetric distributions like a ring, the potential integral is almost trivial because every \(dQ\) is the same distance from the point.
Worked Example — the charged ring (from your notes)
Derivation Ring of charge \(+Q\), radius \(a\), on-axis at distance \(x\)

Step 1 — potential. Every element \(dQ\) is the same distance \(r=\sqrt{x^2+a^2}\) from \(P\), so it comes straight out of the integral:

\[ V=\int\frac{k\,dQ}{\sqrt{x^2+a^2}}=\frac{k}{\sqrt{x^2+a^2}}\int dQ=\frac{kQ}{\sqrt{x^2+a^2}} \]

Step 2 — field. Differentiate along the axis:

\[ E=-\frac{dV}{dx}=-kQ\frac{d}{dx}\big(x^2+a^2\big)^{-1/2}=\frac{kQx}{(x^2+a^2)^{3/2}} \]
Same answer, less workThis matches the result from directly integrating the field in Unit 1 — but the potential route avoided all the vector-component bookkeeping.
Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. If the electric potential is known as a function of position \(x\), the \(x\)-component of the field is:
  • A\(E_x=\int V\,dx\)
  • B\(E_x=+\dfrac{dV}{dx}\)
  • C\(E_x=-\dfrac{dV}{dx}\)
  • D\(E_x=V\cdot x\)
Answer C

The field is the negative gradient of potential: \(E_x=-dV/dx\).

MCQ
Q2. The electric potential at the center of a uniformly charged ring (radius \(a\), charge \(Q\)) is:
  • Azero
  • B\(\dfrac{kQ}{a}\)
  • C\(\dfrac{kQ}{a^2}\)
  • Dinfinite
Answer B

Set \(x=0\) in \(V=\dfrac{kQ}{\sqrt{x^2+a^2}}\): \(V=\dfrac{kQ}{a}\). Note this is nonzero even though the field \(E=0\) at the center — a great reminder that \(V=0\) and \(E=0\) are different conditions.

FRQ
Q3. A ring of radius \(a=0.20\ \text{m}\) carries uniform charge \(Q=+10\ \text{nC}\). Find the electric potential at a point on its axis \(x=0.15\ \text{m}\) from the center.
Solution \(V\approx 3.6\times10^{2}\ \text{V}\)
\[ V=\frac{kQ}{\sqrt{x^2+a^2}}=\frac{(8.99\times10^9)(10\times10^{-9})}{\sqrt{(0.15)^2+(0.20)^2}}=\frac{89.9}{0.25}\approx 3.6\times10^{2}\ \text{V} \]
FRQ
Q4. Starting from \(V=\dfrac{kQ}{\sqrt{x^2+a^2}}\) for a charged ring, derive the on-axis electric field, and state why this method is simpler than integrating the field directly.
Solution \(E=\dfrac{kQx}{(x^2+a^2)^{3/2}}\)
\[ E=-\frac{dV}{dx}=-kQ\frac{d}{dx}(x^2+a^2)^{-1/2}=-kQ\left(-\tfrac12\right)(2x)(x^2+a^2)^{-3/2}=\frac{kQx}{(x^2+a^2)^{3/2}} \]

It is simpler because \(V\) is a scalar: every \(dQ\) contributes \(k\,dQ/r\) with the same \(r\), so there are no vector components or angles to handle. Differentiating the resulting scalar is easy.

Σ

Formula Reference Sheet

Unit 2

Every key equation and constant for electric potential in one place.

Constants
Coulomb constant
\(k=8.99\times10^{9}\ \dfrac{\text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2}{\text{C}^2}\)
Permittivity
\(\varepsilon_0=8.85\times10^{-12}\ \dfrac{\text{C}^2}{\text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2}\)
Elementary charge
\(e=1.60\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}\)
Electron-volt
\(1\ \text{eV}=1.60\times10^{-19}\ \text{J}\)
Electron / proton mass
\(9.11\times10^{-31}\,/\,1.67\times10^{-27}\ \text{kg}\)
Volt
\(1\ \text{V}=1\ \text{J/C}\)
Potential Energy
QuantityEquationNotes
Two point charges\(U_E=\dfrac{kq_1q_2}{r}\)zero at \(r=\infty\); sign from charges
System of charges\(U_{\text{total}}=k\sum\limits_{\text{pairs}}\dfrac{q_iq_j}{r_{ij}}\)scalar sum over all pairs
From potential\(U=qV\)energy of charge \(q\) at potential \(V\)
Change (any field)\(\Delta U=q\,\Delta V\)—
Change (uniform field)\(\Delta U=-qE\,\Delta r\)\(\Delta r\parallel \vec E\)
Electric Potential & Difference
QuantityEquationNotes
Definition\(V=\dfrac{U}{q}\)scalar, units volts (J/C)
Point charge\(V=\dfrac{kq}{r}\)superpose: \(V=\sum kq_i/r_i\)
Charged ring (axis)\(V=\dfrac{kQ}{\sqrt{x^2+a^2}}\)\(V=kQ/a\) at center
General difference\(\Delta V=-\displaystyle\int \vec E\cdot d\vec l\)path-independent
Field from potential\(E=-\dfrac{dV}{dr}\)units V/m = N/C
Uniform field\(\Delta V=-E\,\Delta r\), \(\ E=\dfrac{V}{d}\)parallel plates
Near a point charge\(\Delta V=kq\!\left(\dfrac{1}{r_B}-\dfrac{1}{r_A}\right)\)—
Work, Energy & Speed
QuantityEquationNotes
Work to move a charge\(W=q\,\Delta V\)no change in KE
Speed from rest\(v=\sqrt{\dfrac{2|q\,\Delta V|}{m}}\)energy conservation
Equipotential surface\(W=0\), \(\ \vec E\perp\) surface\(\vec E\) points to lower \(V\)
